doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Linear atelectatic changes in the lingular segments on the left, paravertebrally in the right lower lobe.
A ground-glass nodule of moderate attenuation in the right upper lobe measures up to 1.5 × 1.2 cm (image 53).
A right perifissural calcification measures up to 3 mm.
No pleural effusion.
The trachea and large bronchi are patent; the walls are partially calcified.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass is identified.
A round station 8 lymph node is enlarged to 1.2 cm in short axis.

Heart: No cardiomegaly.
No significant pericardial effusion.
Aortic valve calcification.
High-density linear inclusions in the walls of the coronary arteries - coronary artery stenting of the right coronary artery, left anterior descending artery, and probably circumflex artery - assessment of coronary calcification is limited in this setting.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated; wall calcification is present.
Aberrant right subclavian artery (arteria lusoria).
The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation.
Decreased density of vertebral bodies at T11-L1 to +140 HU.

Upper abdomen: Calcification of the abdominal aortic wall.
High-density inclusions are seen in the stomach (about +900 HU/+1100 HU).
doc_impression:
1. CT appearance of a ground-glass nodule in the right upper lobe: per 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations, follow-up CT is recommended in 6-12 months, then every 2 years until 5 years.

2. Intrathoracic lymphadenopathy of a station 8 lymph node.

3. Coronary artery stenting.

4. Aortic valve calcification.

5. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic and abdominal aorta. Aberrant right subclavian artery (arteria lusoria).

6. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. Decreased vertebral body density at T11-L1.

7. High-density content in the lumen of the stomach.
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Linear atelectatic changes in the lingular segments on the left, in the lung bases. In the right lower lobe there is a paravertebral area of osteophyte-induced fibrosis.
In the lung parenchyma, solid nodules are identified, in series 5, presented as follows:
Right upper lobe:
- A ground-glass nodule measures up to 1.5 × 1.2 cm (image 53).
Right lower lobe:
- A subpleural ground-glass nodule measures up to 5 mm (image 175).
- A part part-solid nodule measures up to 3 mm (image 166).
Left lower lobe:
- A part part-solid nodule measures up to 3 mm (image 159).
A right perifissural calcified nodule measures up to 3 mm, requires no follow-up.
No pleural effusion. The trachea and large bronchi are patent; the walls are partially calcified. Wall thickening and secretion of the segmental and subsegmental bronchi. 

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass is identified. A round para-esophageal node (station 8) is enlarged to 1.2 cm in short axis (image 175).

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No significant pericardial effusion. Aortic valve calcification. Coronary artery calcifications are identified in the left main, left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as severe.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated; wall calcification is present. Aberrant right subclavian artery (arteria lusoria). The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. Decreased density of vertebral bodies at T11-L1 to +140 HU (CT signs of demineralization).

Upper abdomen: Calcification of the abdominal aortic wall.
val_impression:
1. A ground-glass nodule measures 1.5 × 1.2 cm in the right upper lobe. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: Initial chest CT at 6-12 months to confirm persistence. If stable, chest CT every 2 years until 5 years. Part Part-solid and ground-glass nodules in both lungs measure up to 5 mm. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations suggest the following: Follow-up chest CT at 3-6 months to confirm persistence. If stable, consider chest CT at 2 and 4 years. 
Wall thickening and secretion of the segmental and subsegmental bronchi may represent bronchitis.

2. Intrathoracic lymphadenopathy of a para-esophageal lymph node.

3. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as severe. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has known CAD, prior CABG/stent, trauma indications or intraoperative CTs.

4. Aortic valve calcification. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic and abdominal aorta. Aberrant right subclavian artery (arteria lusoria).





DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
val_protocol:
INDICATION: Pulmonary nodule. Follow-up for nodule in lung. History of smoking.

COMPARISON: None available.

TECHNIQUE: Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 5 mGy; total DLP 196 mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 0.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura:
Pulmonary nodules on series 4 are as follows:
Right upper lobe:
- Subsolid perifissural nodule measuring 4 mm on image 30.
- Solid perifissural nodule measuring 5 mm on image 33.
- Solid nodule measuring 4 mm on image 45.
Left upper lobe:
- Solid micronodules measuring up to 2-3 mm.
The nodule in the left lower lobe is not identified on the current examination.
Calcification in the right upper lobe measuring 6 mm on image 81.
Areas of centrilobular emphysema in the upper lobe parenchyma.
Reticular changes in the peripheral lower lobes with a few areas of honeycombing and a few bronchiolectases.
Parenchymal bands in segment 5 of the left lung, unchanged.
No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. No coronary artery calcification.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated. Main pulmonary artery is not dilated. Moderate aortic calcification. Moderate aortic valve calcification.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Severe degenerative changes of the spine - spondylosis, osteochondrosis; moderate spondyloarthrosis.

Upper abdomen: A few calcifications in the splenic parenchyma.
doc_impression:
1. Solid pulmonary nodules measuring less than 6 mm are stable compared with the prior study. According to Fleischner Society 2017 recommendations, additional CT follow-up is not recommended.

2. Findings of fibrosing interstitial lung disease, CT pattern of usual interstitial pneumonia.

3. Mild centrilobular emphysema.

4. Aortic valve calcification.

5. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

6. A few calcifications in the splenic parenchyma - postinflammatory granulomas.
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura:
Pulmonary nodules on series 4 are as follows:
Right upper lobe:
- Solid perifissural nodule measuring 4 mm on image 30 (unchanged).
- Solid perifissural nodule measuring 5 mm on image 33 (unchanged).
- Solid nodule measuring 4 mm on image 45 (unchanged).
Left upper lobe: 
- Ground glass nodule measuring 6 mm on image 134 (unchanged).
Multiple bilateral solid micronodules measuring up to 3 mm (unchanged).
The nodule in the left lower lobe is not identified on the current examination.
ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: No need for further follow-up for patients with high or low risk of lung cancer.
Calcified granuloma in the right upper lobe measuring 6 mm. Mild bilateral centrilobular emphysema. Linear atelectasis, predominantly in the basal segments of the lungs. Reticular changes in the peripheral lower lobes with scattered areas of honeycombing and a few bronchiolectases. There is progression with increased reticulation and the development of bronchiolectases. No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy. Few calcified lymph nodes are noted in the paratracheal group and at the right lung hilum, likely representing prior granulomatous disease.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. No coronary artery calcification. Moderate aortic valve calcification. 

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated. Main pulmonary artery is not dilated. Moderate aortic calcification. 

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Decreased bone density in the vertebrae, most pronounced in the T12 vertebral body, measuring up to +102 HU.

Upper abdomen: A few calcifications in the splenic parenchyma, likely representing prior granulomatous disease. Gallbladder stone measuring 4 × 5 mm (not fully included in the scan range).
val_impression:
1. Typical UIP CT pattern. Progression of interstitial lung disease is noted, with increased reticulation and newly developed bronchiolectases.

2. Pulmonary nodules. No need for further follow-up.

3. Mild centrilobular emphysema. Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria; today's CT may serve as a baseline for future screening. Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has a history of lung cancer, known pulmonary nodules or lung mass, receives routine chest CTs/screening, or is receiving palliative care.

4. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta. Aortic valve calcification.

5. Gallbladder stone.

6. CT signs of bone demineralization.

7. Few calcified lymph nodes, likely representing prior granulomatous disease.

8. Few calcifications in the splenic parenchyma, likely representing prior granulomatous disease.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Lung nodules in series 5 are as follows:
Right upper lobe:
- Complex solid nodule with irregular margins measuring 2.4 × 1.7 cm on image 50, with fluid density in the central portions and gas inclusion.
- Solid nodule with irregular margins measuring 1.8 × 1.3 cm on image 49.
- Micronodule on image 47.
Linear pleural tags are present at the periphery of the right upper lobe lesions.
Areas of centrilobular and paraseptal emphysema, predominantly in the upper lobes.
No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No significant pericardial effusion. Severe coronary calcification is present in the left and right main coronary arteries, anterior descending and circumflex branches of the left coronary artery, and diagonal branch of the anterior descending artery.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated. Main pulmonary artery is dilated to 3.3 cm. Mild calcification of the aortic wall. Mild calcification of the aortic valve.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Severe degenerative changes of the spine: osteochondrosis, spondylosis. Decreased density of vertebral bodies to 100-120 HU.

Upper abdomen: Decreased density of the liver parenchyma to 40 HU.
doc_impression:
1. Solid and complex solid nodules in the right upper lobe, possibly neoplastic. Inflammatory process cannot be excluded. PET/CT may be performed for further characterization.

2. Mild mixed pulmonary emphysema.

3. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as severe. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

4. Aortic valve calcification.

5. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

6. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.3 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

7. Hepatic steatosis.
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Moderate centrilobular and mild paraseptal emphysema, predominantly in the upper lobes. In the right lower lobe there is a paravertebral area of osteophyte-induced fibrosis. Lung nodules in the right upper lobe in series 5 are as follows:
- Cavitary nodule with irregular margins measures 2.5 × 1.6 cm (image 50), with fluid density in the central part, peripheral bronchiectasis and cords to the pleura.
- Solid nodule with irregular margins measures 1.8 × 1.3 cm (image 49), adjacent to the pleura.
No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No significant pericardial effusion. Severe coronary calcification is present in the left and right main coronary arteries, anterior descending and circumflex branches of the left coronary artery, and diagonal branch of the anterior descending artery. Aortic valve calcifications.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated. Main pulmonary artery is dilated to 3.3 cm. Mild calcification of the aortic wall.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the spine. Decreased density of vertebral bodies to 100 HU (CT signs of demineralization).

Upper abdomen: Decreased density of the liver parenchyma to 40 HU (hepatic steatosis).
val_impression:
1. In the right upper lobe, cavitary and solid nodules are present.
Most likely represents an infectious or inflammatory process, although a neoplasm is also possible.

2. Moderate centrilobular and mild paraseptal emphysema. Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria; today's CT may serve as a baseline for future screening. Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has a history of lung cancer, known pulmonary nodules or lung mass, receives routine chest CTs/screening, or is receiving palliative care.

3. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as severe. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Aortic valve calcification. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

4. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.3 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

Lungs and pleura: Linear atelectatic changes in the basal segments bilaterally.
Solid nodules are identified in the lung parenchyma (without change in number and size); some are partially dense; in series 4, they are as follows:
In the right lung:
- In the middle lobe:
  - Nodule measuring up to 4 mm (image 136), perifissural/subpleural.
  - Nodule measuring up to  mm (image ).
  - Nodule measuring up to  mm (image ).
- In the lower lobe:
  - Nodule measuring up to 4 mm (image 140), perifissural.
In the left lung:
- In the upper lobe:
Nodule measuring up to 4 mm (image 162), subpleural.
- In the lower lobe:
  - Nodule measuring up to 4 mm (image 182), subpleural.
  - Nodule measuring up to 4 mm (image 195), subpleural.
Nodule measuring up to 5 mm (image 170), subpleural.
  - Nodule measuring up to 5 mm (image 109), subpleural.
Also, a few perifissural micronodules measuring up to 2-3 mm are present bilaterally.
No pleural effusion.
Trachea and central bronchi are patent; trace mucous contents are present in the tracheal lumen.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion.
Aortic valve calcification.
Severe coronary calcification of the left and right coronary arteries, as well as the left circumflex and left anterior descending arteries (visually with slight increase in extent).

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated; wall calcification.
Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation.
Decreased bone density in the vertebrae at the T11-L2 level to +92 HU.

Upper abdomen: Partially within the field of view, a hypodense lesion in the parenchyma of the middle third of the left kidney with smooth margins, measuring up to 1.2 × 1.1 cm, fluid density; previously not included in the field of view.
Calcification along the splenic capsule (visually unchanged).
Dense diverticula of the colon (a few at the level of examination), without signs of diverticulitis.
Kinking of the gallbladder near the fundus.
doc_impression:
1. Multiple solid pulmonary nodules (predominantly subpleural) without change compared with the prior study. According to the 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations, further follow-up is not required.

2. Severe coronary calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

3. Aortic valve calcification.

4. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

5. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. Decreased vertebral body density at the T11-L2 level.

6. Hypodense lesion in the left kidney of fluid density. According to Management of the Incidental Renal Mass on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee, 2018, CT follow-up is not required. Calcification along the splenic capsule. Colonic diverticula without signs of diverticulitis.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Heterogeneous lung aeration due to alternating areas of mosaic ground-glass opacity and areas of paraseptal and centrilobular emphysema, greater at the apices, and panlobular emphysema along the dorsal surfaces of the bilateral lower lobes. The extent and severity of these changes are unchanged. Small fibrotic bands in the dorsobasal lungs, greater on the right.
Persistent solid pulmonary nodules, most of which are mildly decreased in size:
Pulmonary nodules in series 5 are as follows:
Right middle lobe:
- Solid nodule measuring 4 mm on image 165 (previously the same size).
Right lower lobe:
- Solid perifissural polygonal nodule measuring 6 mm on image 85 (previously the same size) - likely intrapulmonary lymph node.
- Solid nodule with lobulated margins measuring 5 mm on image 134 (previously 5 mm).
- Solid nodule with lobulated margins measuring 8 mm on image 217 (previously the same size).
Left lower lobe:
- Solid nodule with lobulated margins measuring 5 mm on image 85 (previously 5 mm).
- Polygonal subpleural nodule measuring 5 mm on image 143 (previously 5 mm).
No infiltrative changes in the lungs.
Fragmentary bronchial contents are seen within the lumens of bilateral subsegmental bronchi.
No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No significant pericardial effusion. Fluid is seen in the pericardial recesses. Coronary calcification is present in the left main, left anterior descending, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as moderate. Agatston score 166.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated. Calcified plaques are seen in the walls of the aorta and proximal brachiocephalic arteries. Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Degenerative changes of the spine in the imaged field.

Upper abdomen: Unremarkable.
doc_impression:
1. Moderate mixed emphysema, unchanged compared with CT on September 16, 2025.

2. CT signs of exudative bronchitis in the form of bilateral subsegmental mucus plugs, with interval development compared with CT on September 16, 2025.

3. Interstitial pulmonary changes with ground-glass opacity related to the underlying disease, unchanged compared with CT on September 16, 2025.

4. Pulmonary nodules in the right and left lungs measuring up to 7 mm, with decrease in size of a few nodules compared with CT on September 16, 2025. Per Fleischner Society recommendations, follow-up chest CT at 18-24 months is recommended for high-risk patients, and CT at 18-24 months may be considered for low-risk patients.

5. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as moderate. CAC-DRS A2/N3. Unchanged compared with CT on September 16, 2025. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

6. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

7. Moderate degenerative changes of the spine in the imaged field.
